*The Adventures of Captain Hatteras* is Jules Verne's Arctic epic, chronicling a British expedition driven by one man's monomaniacal quest for the [[The North Pole|North Pole]]. It opens with the mysterious sailing of a brig — "the brig *Forward*, Captain K. Z——, Richard Shandon mate, will start from New Prince's Docks for an unknown destination" (ACH-001-002).

Both men are "kidnapped and carried on board the schooner *Ebba*," then confined in a cavern "in the interior of the islet of Back Cup, which is wrongly regarded as an active volcano" (FF-013-022, FF-013-025). Ker Karraje's aim is plain: once he "has obtained possession of Roch's fulgurator... [he] will be in a position to carry on his crimes with complete impunity" (FF-013-023).

It is then fired electrically: "it was by means of electricity that fire was to be set to this mass of gun-cotton," a wire running two miles to "a powerful pile of Bunsen" (FEM-025-005). On [[lincoln-island|Lincoln Island]] the same substance renews the colonists' ammunition when lead and powder run short (MI-030-006).

The lake lies in a low basin fed by rivers: "the river Shari, which eighty miles farther on rolled its impetuous waters into Lake Tchad, was quite distinctly seen." (FWB-030-020) Its highlands divide watersheds, for "the lofty summits of these mountains separate the valley of the Niger from the basin of Lake Tchad." (FWB-029-048)

Its isolation becomes absolute when the land drifts out to sea. A returning traveller is forced to conclude "that it is not so easy to get to Cape Bathurst or to Fort Hope as we could wish." (FC-023-013)

Boston is the great New England seaport that recurs as an American setting across several of Verne's tales. In *Master of the World* it is one of the coastal cities thrown into alarm by an unidentified craft prowling offshore, and its harbour launches pursuit against the "moving body" (MW-005-030) that no one can catch.
